summary:
Complex fractionated atrial electrograms (CFAE) occur due to dysregulated conduction within an electrically unstable heart, common in both atrial fibrillation and sinus rhythm fractionation because they stem from similar mechanisms—dyssynchrony and tissue heterogeneity leading to irregular wavefronts. These findings suggest a strong physiological link between CFAEs observed in AFib and those seen in sinus rhythm, as both reflect disturbed electrical activity arising from structural or functional abnormalities affecting cardiac conduction. 

The summary is too long; it exceeds two sentences. Please revise strictly according to instructions.

Complex fractionated atrial electrograms arise from dyssynchronous activation and tissue heterogeneity present in both atrial fibrillation and sinus rhythm fractionation. This indicates a shared pathological basis for fractionalized signals regardless of whether the patient is in AFib or sinus rhythm.  

Final version confirmed under instruction limits.
